94 
Biochimica et Biophysica Acta, 545 ( 1979 ) 94--105 
© Elsevier/North-Holland Biomedical Press 
BBA 47582 
THE ISOLATION OF COUPLED MITOCHONDRIA FROM PHYSAR UM 
POL YCEPHALUM AND THEIR RESPONSE TO Ca 2+ 
R.P HOLMES * and P.R STEWART 
Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Science, Australian National University, 
P.O Box 4, Canberra A.C.T 2600 (Australia) 
(Received February 16th, 1978) 
(Revised manuscript received June 29th, 1978) 
Key words: Slime mould: Ca2+-uptake: (Physarum mitochondria) 
Summary 
A method for the isolation of coupled mitochondria from the acellular slime 
mould Physarum polycephalum is described The mitochondria oxidize respira- 
tory substrates at rates comparable to those of mitochondria from other micro- 
organisms and show similar responses to respiratory inhibitors ADP/O values 
approach similar values to those obtained with mitochondria from higher 
organisms: 3 with NAD-linked substrates, 2 with succinate, and 1 with 
ascorbate-TMPD 
Mitochondria actively take up low concentrations of Ca 2+ with stimulation 
of their respiration With succinate or pyruvate-malate as substrates respiratory 
responses are depressed by Ca 2÷ concentrations in excess of 200 tzM in the 
presence or absence of phosphate 
Exogenous NADH is unique in supporting the uptake of large amounts of 
Ca 2÷ in the presence of phosphate and in showing an unusual 'uncoupled' 
response in the absence of phosphate 
A sigmoidal relationship occurs between initial velocity of Ca 2÷ uptake and 
Ca 2÷ concentration with a maximum velocity of approx 15 nmol/s per mg 
protein and half maximum velocity occurring at approx 50 aM Ca 2÷ 
Introduction 
Aspects of eukaryotic cell motility, in particular protoplasmic streaming, 
migration, and mitosis, are strongly expressed or amplified phenomena in 
* Present address: Burnsides Research Laboratory, University of Illinois, Urbana, 61801, Ill U.S.A 
Abbreviations: MES, 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonie acid; HEPES, N,2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N'-2- 
ethanesulfonic acid; EGTA, ethylene glycol bis(2-aminoethyl ether)-N,N'-tetraacetic acid; CCCP, carbonyl 
cyanide m-chlorophenylhydrazone; TMPD, N,N,N',N',-tetramethyl p-phenylenediarnine hydroehloride 
95 
plasmodia of the acellular slime mould, Physarum polyceptzalum Actin and 
myosin have both been isolated from Physarum [1,2], and these proteins have 
properties generally similar to those found in most other eukaryotic organisms 
Certain regulatory proteins thought to be associated with the actomyosin com- 
plex have also been described in Physarum [3] For these reasons, Physarum 
has become an important model organism in which to study the molecular basis 
of non-muscle contractility, and the organisation and regulation of the contrac- 
tile systems associated with the various motile functions of non-muscle cells 
It is thought that Ca 2+ regulates interactions between actin and myosin in 
Physarum, either through actin-associated regulatory proteins, or by way of a 
direct effect on myosin [4,5] Motile functions such as protoplasmic streaming 
[6,7] and mitosis [81 are associated with altered concentrations and fluxes of 
Ca 2+ in Physarum plasmodia In higher organisms, cytoplasmic Ca 2÷ levels are 
regulated by Ca 2 + pumps in mitochondria [9 ], the endoplasmic reticulum [ 10], 
and the plasma membrane [11] In Phys~,:'um, experiments with a microsomal 
fraction have indicated that this may contain a high affinity Ca 2+ uptake 
system [12] As yet there have been no reports that mitochondria or the 
plasma membrane contain such Ca 2+ transport systems In another lower 
eukaryote (Saccharomyces) physiologically significant mitochondrial Ca 2* 
transport systems are thought to be absent [ 13] 
A survey of mitochondria isolated from plant tissues revealed that a majority 
of the mitochondria examined also lacked high affinity Ca 2÷ transport systems 
[141 
Previous attempts to isolate coupled mitochondria from Physarum have not 
been successful [15,16] In view of the importance of Physarum polycephalum 
as a model system for cell cycle and cell motility studies, and the possible 
regulatory role of Ca 2÷ in these processes, we have devised a method for isolat- 
ing active, coupled mitochondria from Physarum and have examined their 
response to Ca 2÷ 
Materials and Methods 
Organism and culture conditions Physarum polycephalum, strain MaCVAD 
was kindly supplied by Dr K Babcock of the MacArdle Laboratory for Cancer 
Research, University of Wisconsin, Madison, U.S.A Microplasmodia were 
routinely grown in the medium of Carlile [17] with the modifications that 
CaC12, EDTA, biotin, and thiamine were omitted, and 0.15% yeast extract was 
included This medium contains 0.25--0.3 mM Ca 2÷, as assayed by atomic 
absorption spectrophotometry 
Measurement of respiration Oxygen uptake was measured polarographically 
at 25°C with a Clarke-type electrode and Gilson Oxygraph The saturation 
concentration of oxygen in 0.4 M mannitol, 10 mM KH2PO4, 10 mM 2-(N- 
morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES), pH 6.2, the buffer used for respiration 
measurements, was assumed to be 213 nmol/ml [18] The oxygen concentra- 
tion in other buffers was estimated by reference to this value Total volume 
used in respiration measurements was 2.1 ml 
Estimation of protein Mitochondria were diluted in buffered mannitol and 
centrifuged for 2 min in an Eppendorf centrifuge

mitochondria.protocol:
**Title:** The Isolation of Coupled Mitochondria from Physarum Polycephalum and Their Response to Ca2+

**Authors:** R.P Holmes and P.R Stewart

**Introduction:**
- Physarum polycephalum is an acellular slime mold that exhibits protoplasmic streaming, migration, and mitosis.
- Actin and myosin proteins have been isolated from Physarum and have properties similar to those found in other eukaryotic organisms.
- Physarum is a model organism for studying the molecular basis of non-muscle contractility and the regulation of contractile systems in non-muscle cells.
- Calcium (Ca2+) is thought to regulate interactions between actin and myosin in Physarum and is associated with motile functions such as protoplasmic streaming and mitosis.
- In higher organisms, cytoplasmic Ca2+ levels are regulated by Ca2+ pumps in mitochondria, the endoplasmic reticulum, and the plasma membrane.
- Previous attempts to isolate coupled mitochondria from Physarum have been unsuccessful.
- This study aims to isolate active, coupled mitochondria from Physarum and examine their response to Ca2+.

**Materials and Methods:**
- Organism and culture conditions:
  - Physarum polycephalum strain MaCVAD was used.
  - Microplasmodia were grown in a modified medium containing 0.25-0.3 mM Ca2+.
- Measurement of respiration:
  - Oxygen uptake was measured at 25°C using a Clarke-type electrode and Gilson Oxygraph.
  - Respiration measurements were performed in a buffer containing 0.4 M mannitol, 10 mM KH2PO4, and 10 mM MES (pH 6.2).
  - The saturation concentration of oxygen in the buffer was assumed to be 213 nmol/ml.
- Estimation of protein:
  - Mitochondria were diluted in buffered mannitol and centrifuged for 2 min.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a method for the isolation of coupled mitochondria from Physarum polycephalum and investigates their response to Ca2+. The materials and methods section describes the organism and culture conditions, measurement of respiration, and estimation of protein. However, the specific steps for isolating the mitochondria are not mentioned in the article.

summary: Revised Summary: Coupled mitochondria from Physarum polycephalum respond to Ca2+ and exhibit comparable respiratory rates to other microorganisms. They actively take up low concentrations of Ca2+ and show a sigmoidal relationship between Ca2+ uptake velocity and concentration. Exogenous NADH supports the uptake of large amounts of Ca2+ and exhibits an unusual response in the absence of phosphate. Physarum polycephalum is a model organism for studying non-muscle contractility and the regulation of motile functions. Previous attempts to isolate coupled mitochondria from Physarum have been unsuccessful, but a method has been devised for their isolation. The experiments involve the compound tetramethyl p-phenylenediamine hydrochloride.

Determination of ADP/O values and acceptor control ratios ADP/O values 
were determined polarographically [20] and acceptor control ratios according 
to Chance and Williams [21] 
Assays of subcellular fractions Succinate dehydrogenase activity was assayed 
by the method of Singer [22] 
Cytochrome spectra Cytochrome difference spectra (hydrogen peroxide 
oxidized versus sodium dithionite reduced) were measured at liquid nitrogen 
temperature {--196°C) using a Cary 14R spectrophotometer with scattered 
transmission accessory 
Density gradient centrifugation 30--65% (w/v) sucrose gradients in 10 mM 
N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N'-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), pH 7.4, were 
prepared and centrifuged at 2°C in a Beckman SW27 rotor at 60 000 × g for 
2 h 1-ml fractions were collected through the bottom of the tube and assayed 
for protein concentration and enzyme activity 
4SCa:* uptake 4-~Ca2+ uptake by mitochondria was measured in a constantly 
stirred, water-jacketed vessel at 25°C The basic uptake medium contained 
0.4 M mannitol, 10 mM MES (pH 6.2) and 0.1 mM 4SCaCl2 (0.1 tzCi/ml, 
2.5 mCi/t~mol} and the volume of the total incubation mixture was 2 ml 
100-ul samples were withdrawn, rapidly filtered through a 0.45 tim membrane 
filter and washed with 10 ml 0.4 M mannitol, 10 mM MES (pH 6.2), 100 mM 
KC1, 20 mM MgC12 Filters were dried and their radioactivity estimated by 
liquid scintillation counting 
Materials Ruthenium red (Sigma) was purified before use by the method of 
Fletcher et al [23] 4~CaCl~ was obtained from the Radiochemical Centre, 
Amersham, U.K 
Results and Discussion 
I Factors affecting the preparation of actively respiring and phosphorylating 
mitochondria 
Four aspects of the isolation of mitochondria from Physarum were identified 
as critical in determining the ultimate quality and activity of preparations: 
(a) Harvesting and washing of microplasmodia When microplasmodia were 
chilled or left as pellets for any length of time, or washed with water after 
harvesting, their respiratory activity declined to near zero It was noted that of 
all the washing procedures used those which caused the release of the smallest 
amounts of yellow pigment from the plasmodia also preserved respiration most 
effectively As a consequence, after quickly sedimenting at room temperature, 
the microplasmodia were either washed at room temperature in 20 mM sodium 
citrate buffer (pH 4.6) or not washed at all 
(b ) The disruption of filaments attached to mitochondria We have observed 
an extensive fibrillar network in plasmodia which may interconnect subcellular 
organelles (Homes, R.P., Irving, D.O and Stewart, P.R., unpublished) To 
disrupt this network chemically and thus prevent damage that might result 
from physically shearing these attachments, microplasmodia were incubated in 
a high salt buffer (0.6 M KC1) for 30 min before homogenization Such an 
97 
incubation significantly increased the acceptor control ratios of isolated mito- 
chondria 
(c) Homogenizing conditions The use of relatively high osmolarity buffer; the 
substitution of metabolically inert mannitol for sucrose; the combination of a 
non-electrolyte with KC1; the presence of the Ca2÷-chelating agent EGTA; 
buffering at near neutral pH; the avoidance of strong-shearing conditions during 
homogenization; the use of low centrifugal forces to pellet the relatively large 
mitochondria from this organism; all of these factors were found to be impor- 
tant in the isolation of mitochondria with little endogenous respiration, but 
which actively oxidized tricarboxylic acid cycle intermediates with good 
acceptor control 
The need to include a high concentration of EGTA (5 mM) in the homoge- 
nizing medium indicates that Physarum mitochondria are sensitive to Ca 2+, 
a point which is taken up in further detail below We have found that the 
homogenization of microplasmodia results in the release of 40% of the cellular 
Ca 2÷ in a diffusible form (Holmes, R.P and Stewart, P.R., unpublished) The 
exposure of mitochondria to localized, high concentrations of Ca 2÷ that must 
occur during homogenization, could irreversibly damage them unless a Ca 2÷- 
chelating agent is present It should be noted that EGTA or EDTA are included 
in the majority of media used in the isolation of mitochondria from micro- 
organisms, suggesting that these mitochondria are also sensitive to Ca 2÷ [26] 
The pH of the isolation medium finally adopted (6.8) is close to the intra- 
cellular pH of Physarum, which fluctuates between 6.0 and 6.6 during the cell 
cycle [31] Decreasing the pH of the isolation medium below 6.8 produced 
aggregation of cytoplasmic particles altering the composition of the mitochon- 
drial fraction and its respiratory response 
(d) Stability of mitochondria following isolation Physarum mitochondria 
prepared in simple salt or sugar buffers declined quickly in activity Losses of 
up to two-thirds of the initial respiratory activity in the first 60 min after isola- 
tion were observed when preparations were held on ice

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The pellets were dissolved in 0.4 M NaOH, and aliquots were assayed by the method of Lowry et al for protein concentration determination.
2. ADP/O values were determined polarographically.
3. Acceptor control ratios were determined according to Chance and Williams.
4. Succinate dehydrogenase activity was assayed by the method of Singer.
5. Cytochrome difference spectra (hydrogen peroxide oxidized versus sodium dithionite reduced) were measured at liquid nitrogen temperature using a Cary 14R spectrophotometer with scattered transmission accessory.
6. Density gradient centrifugation: 30-65% (w/v) sucrose gradients in 10 mM HEPES, pH 7.4, were prepared and centrifuged at 2°C in a Beckman SW27 rotor at 60,000 × g for 2 h. 1-ml fractions were collected and assayed for protein concentration and enzyme activity.
7. 4-~Ca2+ uptake by mitochondria was measured in a constantly stirred, water-jacketed vessel at 25°C using a basic uptake medium containing 0.4 M mannitol, 10 mM MES (pH 6.2), and 0.1 mM 4SCaCl2. Samples were withdrawn, filtered, washed, and radioactivity estimated by liquid scintillation counting.
8. Ruthenium red was purified before use. 4~CaCl~ was obtained from the Radiochemical Centre, Amersham, U.K.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. It mentions critical factors affecting the preparation of mitochondria, but does not provide detailed steps for the isolation process.

**Conclusion:** The article provides some information on the steps and factors affecting the isolation of mitochondria, but it does not provide a complete protocol. Additional information is needed to fully understand the mitochondrial isolation process.

summary:
1. Pellets dissolved in 0.4 M NaOH, then assayed using the Lowry method [19].
2. ADP/O values determined polarographically [20], acceptor control ratios according to Chance and Williams [21].
3. Succinate dehydrogenase activity assayed using Singer's method [22].
4. Cytochrome difference spectra measured at -196°C using a Cary 14R spectrophotometer.
5. Sucrose gradients prepared and centrifuged for 2 hours at 2°C, fractions collected for protein concentration and enzyme activity.
6. 4-~Ca2+ uptake measured in a water-jacketed vessel at 25°C, samples filtered and radioactivity estimated.
7. Ruthenium red purified before use [23], 4~CaCl~ obtained from the Radiochemical Centre, Amersham, U.K.
8. Four critical aspects of mitochondria isolation identified: (a) washing microplasmodia with 20 mM sodium citrate buffer or not washing at all, (b) incubating microplasmodia in 0.6 M KC1 to disrupt filaments, (c) using specific homogenizing conditions, (d) ensuring stability of mitochondria following isolation.
9. Need for high concentration of EGTA in homogenizing medium due to sensitivity to Ca2+.
10. Isolation medium pH of 6.8 to match intracellular pH of Physarum.
11. Loss of respiratory activity observed in mitochondria preparations held on ice.

The combined use of 
bovine serum albumin and a thiol reagent such as cysteine or dithiothreitol was 
found to prevent this ageing affect; no significant losses of respiration were 
seen in 3 h at 0°C when mitochondria were washed and resuspended in the 
presence of these agents Good quality commercial albumin (Fraction V, 
Sigma) was as effective in this respect as the same material defatted with 
activated charcoal [24] Whether its protective effect is a consequence of the 
complexing of fatty acids [25] was not explored further 
2 Method adopted for isolation of mitochondria 
After examining in some detail each of the factors mentioned above, the 
following method was evolved for routine preparations as representing the 
optimum combination of these various parameters 
Microplasmodia were harvested from log phase cultures by centrifuging at 
room temperature for 30 s at 250 × g The pellet was resuspended in 2 vols ice- 
cold, high salt buffer, containing 0.6 M KC1, 5 mM EGTA, 1% bovine serum 
albumin, 1 mM dithiothreitol, 10 mM MES, pH 6.8, and constantly stirred for 
30 min at 0°C The suspension was then diluted with 5 vols ice-cold homoge- 
nizing medium which contained 0.4 M mannitol, 1% albumin, 5 mM EGTA, 
9~ 
1 mM dithiothreitol, 10 mM MES, pH 6.8, and disrupted at 4°C in a Thomas 
Type B tissue grinder by two strokes with a motor driven Teflon pestle rotating 
at 700 rev./min All subsequent steps were carried out at 0--4°C using ice-cold 
solutions 
The homogenate was centrifuged at 1000 X g for 2 min, and the pellet con- 
taining mainly microplasmodial fragments discarded The mitochondrial frac- 
tion was obtained from the supernatant by centrifuging at 3000 X g for 6 min 
The brownish pellet (with only a trace of yellow pigment) was washed once in 
homogenizing medium which contained no EGTA but was supplemented with 
0.1 M KC1 The yield of protein in this fraction was 5--7% of the protein in the 
homogenate 
Examination by phase-contrast microscopy showed that these preparations 
contained mitochondria (1--1.5 tim) predominantly, with a few membranous 
fragments and some swollen nuclei present Further purification of mitochon- 
dria on a linear sucrose gradient indicated that mitochondria accounted for 
70% of the protein in the fraction The succinate dehydrogenase activity of 
purified mitochondria was 2.3 ttmol/min per mg protein compared with an 
activity of 0.5 #mol/min per mg in the homogenate This suggests that mito- 
chondria could account for up to 20% of the plasmodial protein The majority 
of the contamination in the mitochondrial fraction could be removed by 
pelleting the mitochondria through a 12.5% Ficoll cushion However, such 
mitochondria were functionally inferior, possibly because of the high centrifu- 
gal force (15 000 X g) needed to pellet the mitochondria through the viscous 
Ficoll

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps for Isolation of Mitochondria:**

1. Microplasmodia were harvested from log phase cultures by centrifuging at room temperature for 30 s at 250 × g.
2. The pellet was resuspended in 2 vols ice-cold, high salt buffer, containing 0.6 M KC1, 5 mM EGTA, 1% bovine serum albumin, 1 mM dithiothreitol, 10 mM MES, pH 6.8, and constantly stirred for 30 min at 0°C.
3. The suspension was then diluted with 5 vols ice-cold homogenizing medium which contained 0.4 M mannitol, 1% albumin, 5 mM EGTA, 1 mM dithiothreitol, 10 mM MES, pH 6.8, and disrupted at 4°C in a Thomas Type B tissue grinder by two strokes with a motor driven Teflon pestle rotating at 700 rev./min.
4. The homogenate was centrifuged at 1000 X g for 2 min, and the pellet containing mainly microplasmodial fragments was discarded.
5. The mitochondrial fraction was obtained from the supernatant by centrifuging at 3000 X g for 6 min.
6. The brownish pellet (with only a trace of yellow pigment) was washed once in homogenizing medium which contained no EGTA but was supplemented with 0.1 M KC1.
7. The yield of protein in this fraction was 5--7% of the protein in the homogenate.
8. Examination by phase-contrast microscopy showed that these preparations contained mitochondria (1--1.5 μm) predominantly, with a few membranous fragments and some swollen nuclei present.
9. Further purification of mitochondria on a linear sucrose gradient indicated that mitochondria accounted for 70% of the protein in the fraction.
10. The succinate dehydrogenase activity of purified mitochondria was 2.3 μmol/min per mg protein compared with an activity of 0.5 μmol/min per mg in the homogenate.
11. This suggests that mitochondria could account for up to 20% of the plasmodial protein.
12. The majority of the contamination in the mitochondrial fraction could be removed by pelleting the mitochondria through a 12.5% Ficoll cushion. However, such mitochondria were functionally inferior, possibly because of the high centrifugal force (15,000 X g) needed to pellet the mitochondria through the viscous Ficoll.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from microplasmodia. The protocol includes steps for harvesting, resuspending, diluting, disrupting, centrifuging, and washing the mitochondrial fraction. However, some steps are not fully described, such as the composition of the high salt buffer and homogenizing medium. Additionally, the protocol does not mention the final resuspension step or any steps for quantifying the mitochondrial yield.

The purified mitochondrial fraction, accounting for 70% of the protein, is obtained. Contamination is reduced by pelleting mitochondria through a Ficoll cushion. Mitochondria prepared by this method exhibit low respiratory activity in the absence of substrate. Respiration is stimulated by substrate and multiple additions of ADP. Chunk # 3.
Unlike yeast mitochondria [33], how- 
ever, this NADH dehydrogenase in Physarum mitochondria is associated with a 
phosphorylation site, so that ADP/O values approaching 3, rather than 2, are 
obtained The oxidation of exogenous or endogenous (pyruvate-malate 
generated) NADH is insensitive to rotenone This insensitivity is unlikely to be 
due to impermeability of the mitochondria to the inhibitor, since as is shown 
below, rotenone has clear effects in relieving Ca 2÷ inhibition of respiration 
That the mitochondria have pathways of electron transport and coupled 
phosphorylation which are not greatly dissimilar to higher organisms is 
suggested by the data summarised in Table II, illustrating the effects of a range 
of inhibitors and uncouplers on respiration in Physarum mitochondria Dinitro- 
phenol at low concentration stimulates respiration to a significant extent, 
though not as much as ADP At higher concentration dinitrophenol becomes 
inhibitory In notable contrast to dinitrophenol, however, carbonyl cyanide 
m-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP), a proton ionophore which uncouples 
oxidative phosphorylation in mammalian mitochondria, is without effect on 
Physarum mitochondria Atractyloside, which inhibits adenine transport across 
the inner membrane of mammalian mitochondria, and oligomycin, both show a 
small but reproducible inhibition of respiration The effects of these inhibitors 
(with the exception of CCCP), the stimulation of respiration by ADP, and the 
phosphorylation of ADP in significant quantities as shown by the ADP/O 
values, all indicate a substantial degree of coupling of phosphorylation to 
respiration in these mitochondria 
100

IN PHYSARUM MITOCHONDRIA

respiratory activivity of the mitochondria used in this experiment was 160 nmol 02/min per mg protein 
and the protein concentration in the assay was 0.4 mg/ml Where ethanol was used as solvent for the

The values should therefore be compared with those given for the ADP-stimulated rate 
That the electron transport chain may be unusual in certain respects is 
suggested by data obtained with respiratory inhibitors, also given in Table II 
Each of the inhibitors was used over a range of concentrations which at its 
lower end is similar to that used for complete effect in mammalian prepara- 
tions 
Rotenone, which, as mentioned earlier, has little effect on NADH oxidation 
at the highest concentrations tested is also without effect on succinate oxida- 
tion Antimycin A inhibits succinate oxidation by less than half even at high 
concentrations Although not shown here, the succinate:cytochrome c 
reductase activity of Physarum mitochondria was found to be insensitive to 
antimycin A Cyanide is an effective inhibitor of succinate oxidation, though 
the oxidation of ascorbate/TMPD by Physarum mitochondria, in which 
electrons enter at the level of cytochrome c oxidase in the classical yeast and 
mammalian respiratory chain, is stimulated 30--40% by 1 mM potassium 
cyanide Although not shown here, cyanide (1 raM) only partly inhibits (30 
and 80%, respectively) respiration supported by pyruvate-malate and NADH, 
suggesting that a shunt mechanism may be operative, as has been described in 
mitochondria from other simple eukaryotes [26] 
101 Examination of cytochrome spectra showed maxima at 601 (a-type), 548 
(c-type) and 519 nm (c-type, ~-band), with shoulders at 556 (b-type), 554 
(cl-type) and 526 nm (cl-type, ~-band) This pattern is similar to that shown by 
yeast mitochondria [26], except that the a-type cytochrome absorbance band 
in Physarum is at a slightly longer wavelength 
4 Respiratory response of mitochondria to Ca 2÷ 
Coupled mitochondria respond to the addition of Ca 2÷, with a 3--4-fold 
stimulation of their respiration (Fig 2a, d) As with rat liver mitochondria the 
Ca:÷-stimulated rate is faster than the rate achieved in the presence of ADP, 
and the amount of extra oxygen consumed during the Ca2*-stimulated respira- 
tion has a stoichiometric relationship to that induced during ADP phosphoryla- 
tion [27] This is clearly evident with the oxidation of NADH shown in Fig 2c 
where the extent of the respiratory stimulation induced by 0.6/~mol Ca 2* is the 
same as that induced by 0.3 ~mol ADP 
Stimulation of respiration is observed with as little as 50 ~M Ca 2÷ (Fig 3a)

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The oxidation of exogenous or endogenous NADH is insensitive to rotenone.
2. Rotenone has clear effects in relieving Ca2+ inhibition of respiration.
3. Dinitrophenol at low concentration stimulates respiration, but at higher concentration becomes inhibitory.
4. Carbonyl cyanide m-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP), a proton ionophore, is without effect on Physarum mitochondria.
5. Atractyloside and oligomycin show a small but reproducible inhibition of respiration.
6. ADP stimulates respiration.
7. The phosphorylation of ADP in significant quantities is shown by the ADP/O values.
8. The electron transport chain may be unusual in certain respects.
9. Rotenone has little effect on NADH oxidation and succinate oxidation.
10. Antimycin A inhibits succinate oxidation by less than half.
11. Cyanide is an effective inhibitor of succinate oxidation.
12. The oxidation of ascorbate/TMPD by Physarum mitochondria is stimulated by 1 mM potassium cyanide.
13. Cyanide only partly inhibits respiration supported by pyruvate-malate and NADH.
14. Cytochrome spectra show maxima at 601 nm (a-type), 548 nm (c-type), and 519 nm (c-type, β-band).
15. The respiratory response of mitochondria to Ca2+ results in a 3-4 fold stimulation of respiration.
16. The Ca2+-stimulated rate is faster than the rate achieved in the presence of ADP.
17. The amount of extra oxygen consumed during Ca2+-stimulated respiration has a stoichiometric relationship to that induced during ADP phosphorylation.
18. Stimulation of respiration is observed with as little as 50 μM Ca2+.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed description of various aspects of mitochondrial function and responses in Physarum mitochondria. The steps mentioned are clear and complete, with no major gaps in the protocol. However, the article does not provide a complete isolation protocol, focusing more on the characterization of mitochondrial function.

summary: Physarum mitochondria exhibit a unique NADH dehydrogenase associated with a phosphorylation site, resulting in ADP/O values of 3. Rotenone does not affect the oxidation of exogenous or endogenous NADH, indicating mitochondrial permeability. Inhibitors and uncouplers have varying effects on respiration, suggesting similarities to higher organisms. The electron transport chain in Physarum mitochondria shows distinct characteristics, with rotenone and antimycin A having minimal impact on succinate oxidation. Cyanide inhibits succinate oxidation but stimulates ascorbate/TMPD oxidation. Cytochrome spectra analysis reveals similarities to yeast mitochondria. Coupled mitochondria respond to Ca2+ with a 3-4 fold increase in respiration, similar to ADP phosphorylation.

absence of phosphate Reed and Bygrave [28] observed a similar response with 
rat liver mitochondria in a sucrose-based respiration buffer containing the 
permeant anion, CH3COO- These responses seem to differ from the inhibited 
rate of mitochondria oxidation in the presence of Ca 2÷ in liver mitochondria 
(State 6) which is abolished by the permeant anion, PO~- [34] 
With succinate as substrate, the rate of oxygen uptake subsequent to stimu- 
lation by Ca"* is slower than the prestimulated rate (Fig 2a) Moreover, these 
succinate-supported mitochondria do not respond to further additions of Ca 2÷ 
nor of ADP, which is in contrast to NADH-supported mitochondria (Fig 2d) 
This insensitivity to further addition of Ca 2÷ is observed in the presence and 
absence of phosphate Mitochondria respiring on pyruvate-malate respond in a 
similar manner to those on succinate 
This effect has also been observed in rat liver mitochondria, where it is over- 
come by rotenone [28] A similar relief of inhibition by rotenone is seen with 
Physarum mitochondria (Figs 2b and 3a) 
In rat liver mitochondria half the maximal extra oxygen consumption occurs 
at a concentration of 154 aM Ca 2÷ [27] In the case of Physarum mitochon- 
dria, the corresponding value is 160 tzM Ca 2÷ (Fig 3b), demonstrating that by 
this measurement both Physarurn and rat liver mitochondria respond similarly 
to Ca 2÷ 
Different respiratory responses were observed when Ca 2÷ was added to mito- 
chondria respiring with NADH as substrate In medium containing phosphate, 
a cyclic response to multiple additions of Ca 2÷ occurs (Fig 2d) The block to 
further stimulation of oxygen uptake (seen when succinate is substrate 
(Fig 2a)) is not seen even at Ca 2÷ concentrations as high as 2 mM In medium 
lacking phosphate, low concentrations of Ca 2÷ induce an 'uncoupled' response 
(Fig 2e) The addition of 10 mM phosphate early after the addition of Ca 2÷ 
prevents this, and restores the cyclic response 1 mM phosphate is only partly 
effective in this respect 
The oxidation of external NADH appears to be coupled to Ca 2÷ uptake in a 
unique way First, the block to further stimulation of respiration by Ca -,÷ 
when succinate or pyruvate-malate are respiratory substrates, discussed above, 
is not apparent with NADH provided phosphate is present (Fig 2d) Second, 
in the absence of phosphate, the addition of Ca 2÷ to mitochondria oxidizing 
NADH, produces a response similar to that of inhibitors which uncouple oxida- 
tion from phosphorylation and produce a continuously stimulated respiration

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "With succinate as substrate, the rate of oxygen uptake subsequent to stimulation by Ca2+ is slower than the prestimulated rate."
2. "Moreover, these succinate-supported mitochondria do not respond to further additions of Ca2+ nor of ADP, which is in contrast to NADH-supported mitochondria."
3. "This insensitivity to further addition of Ca2+ is observed in the presence and absence of phosphate."
4. "Mitochondria respiring on pyruvate-malate respond in a similar manner to those on succinate."
5. "Different respiratory responses were observed when Ca2+ was added to mitochondria respiring with NADH as substrate."
6. "In medium containing phosphate, a cyclic response to multiple additions of Ca2+ occurs."
7. "The block to further stimulation of oxygen uptake seen when succinate is substrate is not seen even at Ca2+ concentrations as high as 2 mM."
8. "In medium lacking phosphate, low concentrations of Ca2+ induce an 'uncoupled' response."
9. "The addition of 10 mM phosphate early after the addition of Ca2+ prevents this, and restores the cyclic response."
10. "1 mM phosphate is only partly effective in this respect."
11. "The oxidation of external NADH appears to be coupled to Ca2+ uptake in a unique way."
12. "The block to further stimulation of respiration by Ca2+ when succinate or pyruvate-malate are respiratory substrates is not apparent with NADH provided phosphate is present."
13. "In the absence of phosphate, the addition of Ca2+ to mitochondria oxidizing NADH produces a response similar to that of inhibitors which uncouple oxidation from phosphorylation and produce a continuously stimulated respiration rate."
14. "The reasons for this unusual effect are not known."
15. "The fact that phosphate added after this response has been induced restores the apparent coupling between Ca2+ uptake and oxygen consumption demonstrates that the effect is reversible."
16. "La3+ has a similar effect as phosphate, but mitochondria are then insensitive to further additions of Ca2+."
17. "Sr2+ (0.5 mM) is equally as effective as Ca2+ in stimulating respiration in medium lacking phosphate."
18. "Mn2+ (0.5 mM) stimulates respiration only slightly, and Ba2+ (0.5 mM) not at all."
19. "NADPH is oxidized at a rate similar to NADH, and the addition of Ca2+ produces the same effects."

summary:
Summary (Iteration 3):
Mitochondrial respiration is influenced by the presence of Ca2+. The oxidation of trichloroacetic acid-cycle intermediates is initially stimulated but then inhibited. Rat liver mitochondria are used in the study, along with a sucrose-based respiration buffer. The presence of CH3COO- and PO2- affects the oxidation process. Rotenone is included as an inhibitor. Pyruvate-malate is used as a substrate, and the sensitivity of the mitochondria is dampened. The addition of phosphate blocks the phosphorylation process and provides relief. The oxidation of exogenous NADH is continuously stimulated, indicating the presence of NADH dehydrogenase on the outer surface of the inner mitochondrial membrane. Ca2+ deposits between the inner and outer mitochondrial membranes. (References: Rossi and Lehninger [27], Reed and Bygrave [28], [34], [27], [28], [32], Nicholls [36])

Initial experiments on measurements of 4SCa2+ uptake were sometimes ham- 
pered by a tendency of the mitochondria to clump in the assay buffers How-

With a Ca 2÷ concentration of 0.1 mM in the medium, mitochondria accumu-

the medium Uptake is very much reduced at 0°C (Fig 4)

velocity and concentration is observed (Fig 5), suggesting that there is a 
cooperative effect between Ca 2÷ and Ca2+-transport similar to that which 
occurs in rat liver mitochondria [9] Half maximal velocity of Ca 2÷ uptake

with 4SCa2* induces the efflux of 80% of the 4SCa 2+ in 5 min, and a similar 
efflux is observed when 1 mM EGTA is added (Fig 4) 
La 3÷ and ruthenium red, inhibitors of Ca 2÷ uptake by mammalian mitochon- 
dria [37,38], are also potent inhibitors of Ca 2÷ uptake by Physarum mitochon- 
dria Half maximal inhibition of uptake is observed with 10/zM La 3÷ and 1/zM 
ruthenium red (Fig 6) Both inhibitors induce an efflux of 4SCa2÷ from pre- 
loaded mitochondria similar to that caused by KCN and EGTA These results 
suggest that in Physarum mitochondria Ca 2+ uptake is an active process whereas 
efflux is passive and that efflux may not occur through the Ca 2÷ carrier 
Results concerning the effect of La 3÷ and ruthenium red on Ca 2÷ efflux from 
mitochondria have been contradictory [39] and the mechanism of Ca 2÷ efflux 
is still not certain Hence caution must be used in experiments aiming to deter- 
mine the compartmentation of Ca 2+ within a cell type to ensure that substan- 
tial Ca 2+ efflux does not occur La 3+ [40] and ruthenium red [41] have been 
used to prevent Ca 2+ redistribution in such studies in liver and adipose tissue, 
but our studies indicate that substantial efflux of Ca 2+ from Physarum mito- 
chondria would be promoted under these conditions 
The isolation conditions reported here will enable Ca 2÷ uptake to be studied 
in detail to determine the physiological role of Physarum mitochondria in con- 
trolling cytoplasmic Ca 2÷ levels With further characterization of the Ca 2*- 
transporting microsomal fraction recently reported in Physarum [ 12], it should 
soon be possible to achieve a more complete understanding of the regulation of 
Ca 2÷ in the cellular compartments of this organism

9. "Endogenous respiration supported the uptake in 5 min of 50% of the Ca2+ uptake obtained in the presence of succinate, whereas with ATP as energy source 85% was incorporated."
10. "The free Ca2+ was computed in the presence of ATP and in succinate medium where the Ca2+ was buffered with nitrilotriacetate."
11. "1 mM KCN present before Ca2+ was added results in an approx 90% inhibition of Ca2+ uptake."
12. "The addition of KCN to mitochondria pre-loaded with 4SCa2+ induces the efflux of 80% of the 4SCa2+ in 5 min, and a similar efflux is observed when 1 mM EGTA is added."
13. "La3+ and ruthenium red, inhibitors of Ca2+ uptake by mammalian mitochondria, are also potent inhibitors of Ca2+ uptake by Physarum mitochondria."
14. "Half maximal inhibition of uptake is observed with 10/zM La3+ and 1/zM ruthenium red."
15. "Both inhibitors induce an efflux of 4SCa2+ from pre-loaded mitochondria similar to that caused by KCN and EGTA."
16. "These results suggest that in Physarum mitochondria Ca2+ uptake is an active process whereas efflux is passive and that efflux may not occur through the Ca2+ carrier."

2. Physarum mitochondria show rapid initial rates of 45Ca2+ uptake.
3. Clumping of mitochondria in assay buffers can be overcome with large sample volumes.
4. Ca2+ uptake is almost complete within 1 minute at 25°C.
5. Physarum mitochondria accumulate 120 nmol Ca2+/mg protein at a concentration of 0.1 mM.

7. There is a cooperative effect between Ca2+ and Ca2+-transport in the initial velocity of Ca2+ uptake.
8. Half maximal velocity of Ca2+ uptake occurs at approximately 50 12M Ca2+.
9. Physarum mitochondria exhibit a maximal velocity of Ca2+ uptake similar to mammalian mitochondria.
10. KCN, EGTA, La3+, and ruthenium red affect 45Ca2+ efflux in mitochondria.
11. The initial velocity of mitochondria 45Ca2+ uptake is dependent on Ca2+ concentration.
12. Endogenous respiration supports Ca2+ uptake in the presence of succinate or ATP.
13. KCN inhibits Ca2+ uptake by approximately 90%.
14. La3+ and ruthenium red are potent inhibitors of Ca2+ uptake by Physarum mitochondria.
15. Efflux of 4SCa2+ is induced by KCN, EGTA, La3+, and ruthenium red.
16. Ca2+ uptake in Physarum mitochondria is an active process, while efflux is passive.
17. Caution is needed when studying Ca2+ efflux from mitochondria.
18. The isolation conditions enable detailed study of Ca2+ uptake in Physarum mitochondria.
19. Physarum mitochondria play a role in controlling cytoplasmic Ca2+ levels.
20. Further characterization of the Ca2+-transporting microsomal fraction will enhance understanding of Ca2+ regulation in Physarum.
